planting.note: Surface sow in spring in consistently wet to saturated, acidic soil at pond margins, bogs, or shallow seeps in partial shade; seeds require light for germination and must not dry out — press onto wet substrate and maintain standing shallow water or saturated conditions throughout establishment.

directions: Lobelia boykinii is a federally threatened native lobelia of the southeastern coastal plain, restricted in the wild to shallow ponds, seepage bogs, and wet glades in the Carolinas, Georgia, and Florida where it serves as an important indicator of high-quality wetland habitat; it produces pale blue to nearly white flowers on stems that stand upright from shallow standing water or saturated soil from summer into early fall. Because of its federal threatened status, all plants in cultivation must come from legally obtained nursery-propagated stock — never collect from wild populations. Establish in the wettest part of a rain garden, bog garden, or at the margins of a wildlife pond in partial shade; consistent shallow inundation or saturated soil is essential and the plant will not persist through drought.

planting.note: Surface sow in spring in consistently moist to wet, very acidic, nutrient-poor soil in partial shade; this species occurs naturally in pitcher plant bogs and wet pine flatwoods where soils are strongly acidic and low in fertility — avoid rich, amended soils which favor competing vegetation over this specialist.

directions: Lobelia paludosa is one of the most ecologically distinctive native lobelias of the Southeast, notable for bearing white flowers in a genus where blue or red blooms are nearly universal; it inhabits wet pine flatwoods, pitcher plant bogs, and acidic seepage areas from Virginia through Florida where it blooms from late summer into fall. Unlike most garden lobelias, it thrives specifically in the very acidic, consistently saturated, low-fertility soils of coastal plain wetlands, making it an exceptional botanical choice for authentic southeastern bog gardens or naturalized wet pine savanna plantings. Surface sow without covering seeds in spring onto wet, strongly acidic substrate; do not fertilize or amend soil with compost, as lean acidic conditions are what give this plant a competitive edge in its specialized habitat.

planting.note: Surface sow in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial sun; Florida lobelia is a warm-season perennial adapted to the subtropical Gulf Coast climate and performs best where summer heat and humidity are consistently high — it is less cold-tolerant than most native lobelias and needs protection from hard freezes in zone 8.

directions: Lobelia floridana is a Florida and Gulf Coast endemic found in moist to wet flatwoods, swamp margins, and roadside ditches, where it produces blue flowers on branching stems in the fall — a critical bloom period for late-migrating pollinators and hummingbirds along the Gulf Coast flyway. It often behaves as a short-lived perennial, freely self-seeding in moist, disturbed soils to perpetuate itself in a favorable site; in warmer zones it may persist for several years without replanting. Surface sow in spring in consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in a site that stays wet through the summer growing season, and allow plants to set seed before cutting back to encourage naturalization.

planting.note: Surface sow in spring in moist to seasonally wet, moderately acidic soil in partial sun; shortleaf lobelia is characteristic of the longleaf pine ecosystem and performs best in open, fire-maintained settings with low competition from aggressive grasses or invasive shrubs.

directions: Lobelia brevifolia is a coastal plain endemic of the southeastern United States, characteristic of longleaf pine upland bogs, pitcher plant savannas, and seepage slopes where it is distinguished from similar lobelias by its notably short, reduced leaves — the feature that gives it its name; it produces blue flowers on erect stems in late summer and fall. This species is a high-fidelity indicator of intact longleaf pine ecosystem habitats and is rarely encountered outside of conservation-quality remnant sites, making it a meaningful ecological garden plant for those working to restore or reconstruct the rapidly disappearing longleaf pine savanna plant community. Sow surface seeds in spring in moderately acidic, moist to seasonally wet soil with good light penetration; avoid heavy mulching or crowding with taller competing vegetation.

common_name: Two-flowered Dwarf Dandelion, Orange Dwarf Dandelion

planting.note: Sow in fall in well-drained to moderately moist, average to slightly acidic soil in partial shade; cold stratification through the winter naturally breaks seed dormancy and promotes strong germination in early spring — fall direct sowing in place is the most reliable establishment method.

directions: Krigia biflora is a cheerful, underused native perennial of woodland margins, forest clearings, and disturbed open areas across eastern North America from Kansas to New England and south into the Appalachians, producing bright orange-yellow, dandelion-like flower heads on branching, leafy stems from late spring into summer — an unusually long bloom season for a member of the chicory family. Unlike the common dandelion, it tolerates meaningful shade and thrives at the transition zone between open lawn and shaded woodland edge, making it an excellent native substitute in naturalistic or no-mow plantings where other sun-loving wildflowers struggle. The milky-sapped stems branch repeatedly after the first blooms, extending the flowering display, and the plant gradually spreads by seed to form attractive loose colonies; direct sow in fall and allow seeds to overwinter naturally for best results.
